title: Why some institutions need to fail in public
summary: Argue that when institutions such as banks, government agencies, or media organisations fail visibly and publicly, the exposure serves an important democratic function.
topic: Why some institutions need to fail in public
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that when institutions — banks, government agencies, media organisations, or universities — fail visibly, the public failure serves an important function that quiet internal reform cannot. You may refer to real examples such as the 2008 banking crisis, the UK phone-hacking scandal, or the collapse of Enron. Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: When Lehman Brothers collapsed in September 2008, the images of bankers carrying boxes were broadcast around the world…
What the public failure of a bank or a newspaper achieves is something that an internal audit can never…
Granted, visible institutional failure carries real costs: markets freeze, trust collapses, and innocent employees…
The question is not whether failure is desirable but whether concealment is worse than…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự thao túng cơ quan quản lý
lợi ích cá nhân / nhóm lợi ích
thiệt hại phụ / thiệt hại ngoài ý muốn

hintVocab.phrase: to be swept under the carpet
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị che giấu / bị ém nhẹm
hành động mà không bị trừng phạt
được che chắn khỏi sự giám sát
bình thường hóa hành vi sai trái
đòi hỏi thay đổi cơ cấu

sampleText:
When Lehman Brothers collapsed in September 2008, the images of employees leaving the Manhattan offices with cardboard boxes were broadcast around the world. For many observers, this was the visual confirmation of what had been hidden for years: that the financial system had been built on risk that its own managers had not understood, or had chosen not to acknowledge. The failure was catastrophic. It was also necessary.

What the public collapse of a major institution achieves is something that an internal audit rarely can: it forces a reckoning that cannot be managed, minimised, or quietly settled. Private failure — the bank that is rescued before anyone notices, the scandal resolved through a confidential settlement — produces no lasting change because it creates no lasting pressure. The people responsible face no real public accountability; the conditions that produced the failure are never named; and the institution, shielded from external scrutiny, continues operating under the same incentive structures that caused the problem in the first place.

Granted, public failure is genuinely damaging in ways that matter. The 2008 crisis destroyed savings, ended careers, and drove economies into recession for years. The UK phone-hacking scandal, when it finally became public in 2011, did not only end Rupert Murdoch's News of the World — it drew in police officers, politicians, and private investigators who had all benefited from the arrangement. Innocent people were caught in the collapse. This is not a trivial cost, and it would be dishonest to pretend that exposure always lands cleanly on those who deserve it.

Even so, the alternative — concealment, internal review, or quiet negotiation — has its own costs, and these tend to be paid by the public over a much longer period. Enron's accounting fraud was known inside the company long before it became news; the delay between misconduct and exposure meant that thousands of employees lost their pensions before anyone could act. When misconduct is shielded from public view, it does not simply wait to be found — it is normalised, extended, and eventually embedded in the culture of the institution.

Public failure is not something to be celebrated. But it is something to be preferred over the alternative: a managed concealment that protects reputations at the expense of reform. The demand for structural change — in regulation, in leadership, in culture — can only be sustained when the public understands what actually went wrong. Visibility is the condition of accountability, and accountability is the condition of trust.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to contrast public and private failure
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the public collapse of a major institution achieves is something that an internal audit rarely can' dùng cấu trúc 'what X achieves is Y' để đặt kết luận chính — sự khác biệt quan trọng giữa sụp đổ công khai và kiểm toán nội bộ — ở cuối câu, nơi nó tạo ra đối lập sắc nét nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the public collapse achieves is something that an internal audit rarely can' uses a what-cleft to elevate the key distinction — public accountability versus private audit — to the end of the sentence, where it carries the most argumentative weight.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive series to describe what happens to concealed misconduct
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'it is normalised, extended, and eventually embedded in the culture of the institution' dùng ba tính từ bị động liên tiếp (normalised, extended, embedded) để mô tả một chuỗi leo thang — mỗi động từ mạnh hơn cái trước — mà không cần xác định ai chịu trách nhiệm, ám chỉ rằng quá trình này là tự động và khó cưỡng lại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'is normalised, extended, and eventually embedded' uses a passive series in ascending order of severity to describe how hidden misconduct escalates without naming responsible individuals, suggesting an impersonal systemic process.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi Lehman Brothers sụp đổ vào tháng Chín năm 2008, hình ảnh nhân viên rời văn phòng Manhattan với những hộp giấy carton được phát sóng khắp thế giới. Đối với nhiều người quan sát, đây là sự xác nhận trực quan về những gì đã bị che giấu trong nhiều năm: rằng hệ thống tài chính đã được xây dựng trên rủi ro mà chính các nhà quản lý không hiểu, hoặc đã chọn không thừa nhận. Sự thất bại thật thảm khốc. Và nó cũng cần thiết.

Điều mà sự sụp đổ công khai của một tổ chức lớn đạt được là điều mà một cuộc kiểm toán nội bộ hiếm khi có thể làm: nó buộc phải có một sự tính sổ không thể bị quản lý, giảm thiểu hay giải quyết yên lặng. Thất bại riêng tư — ngân hàng được giải cứu trước khi ai đó nhận ra, vụ bê bối được giải quyết qua thỏa thuận bảo mật — không tạo ra thay đổi lâu dài vì nó không tạo ra áp lực lâu dài. Những người có trách nhiệm không phải chịu trách nhiệm giải trình công khai thực sự; các điều kiện tạo ra sự thất bại không bao giờ được nêu tên; và tổ chức, được che chắn khỏi sự giám sát bên ngoài, tiếp tục hoạt động theo cùng các cơ cấu khuyến khích đã gây ra vấn đề ngay từ đầu.

Công nhận, sự thất bại công khai thực sự gây hại theo những cách quan trọng. Cuộc khủng hoảng năm 2008 đã phá hủy tiết kiệm, chấm dứt sự nghiệp, và đẩy các nền kinh tế vào suy thoái trong nhiều năm. Vụ bê bối nghe lén điện thoại của Anh, khi cuối cùng trở thành tin tức vào năm 2011, không chỉ kết thúc tờ News of the World của Rupert Murdoch — nó kéo theo cảnh sát, chính trị gia, và điều tra viên tư nhân đã hưởng lợi từ thỏa thuận này. Những người vô tội bị cuốn vào sự sụp đổ. Đây không phải là chi phí tầm thường, và sẽ là không trung thực khi giả vờ rằng việc phơi bày luôn rơi đúng vào những người xứng đáng.

Dẫu vậy, lựa chọn thay thế — che giấu, xem xét nội bộ, hay đàm phán yên lặng — có chi phí riêng của nó, và những chi phí này thường được trả bởi công chúng trong một khoảng thời gian dài hơn nhiều. Gian lận kế toán của Enron đã được biết trong công ty từ lâu trước khi nó trở thành tin tức; sự chậm trễ giữa hành vi sai trái và việc phơi bày có nghĩa là hàng nghìn nhân viên mất lương hưu của họ trước khi ai có thể hành động. Khi hành vi sai trái được che chắn khỏi tầm nhìn công cộng, nó không chỉ đơn giản là chờ đợi để được tìm thấy — nó được bình thường hóa, mở rộng, và cuối cùng được nhúng vào văn hóa của tổ chức.

Sự thất bại công khai không phải là điều cần được tôn vinh. Nhưng đó là điều cần được ưu tiên hơn so với lựa chọn thay thế: một sự che giấu có quản lý bảo vệ danh tiếng bằng cái giá của cải cách. Yêu cầu thay đổi cơ cấu — trong quy định, lãnh đạo, văn hóa — chỉ có thể được duy trì khi công chúng hiểu điều gì thực sự đã xảy ra. Tính hiển thị là điều kiện của trách nhiệm giải trình, và trách nhiệm giải trình là điều kiện của niềm tin.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to contrast public collapse with private audit?
options: Public failure does more than an internal audit because it forces accountability.
What the public collapse of a major institution achieves is something that an internal audit rarely can.
It is the public that achieves what an internal audit cannot when an institution collapses.
What internal audits rarely achieve is the collapse of major public institutions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X achieves is Y' focuses on what public failure uniquely accomplishes, holding the key contrast (internal audit's inadequacy) until the end.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What X achieves is something that Y rarely can'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive series correctly describes how hidden misconduct escalates?
options: it normalises, extends, and eventually embeds itself in the culture
it is normalised, extended, and eventually embedded in the culture of the institution
they normalised, extended, and embedded it in the culture of the institution
the institution normalises it, then extends it, then embeds it in culture
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive 'is normalised, extended, and embedded' removes individual agents and suggests a depersonalised, systemic escalation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses passive throughout without naming who is responsible?

options: When a government successfully regulates a powerful industry
When a regulator is arrested for corruption
When regulated industries exert so much influence that the regulator begins serving their interests instead of the public's
When a regulatory body is dissolved by parliament
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Regulatory capture' describes the process by which an agency meant to act in the public interest instead advances the interests of the industry it is supposed to regulate.
mcQuestions.hint: The 'capture' is of the regulator — not of criminals, but of the watchdog.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'whitewash' mean in the context of institutional failures?
options: A formal judicial inquiry into wrongdoing
A decorating project in a government building
A superficial investigation that conceals rather than reveals the truth
A process of restoring public confidence through transparency
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whitewash' refers to a cover-up — a process that appears to investigate but actually conceals the real facts.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of painting over something dirty to make it look clean.

mcQuestions.prompt: What concession does the essay make about public institutional failure?
options: Public failure always leads to better outcomes in the long run.
Public failure damages innocent people and economies, and exposure does not always target those responsible.
Public failure is only damaging when it involves banks, not other institutions.
Public failure is harmless if the government responds quickly.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that public failure causes genuine harm — destroying savings, ending careers, catching innocent people — and that exposure 'does not always land cleanly on those who deserve it'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What happened at Enron that the essay uses as an example?
options: Enron's executives demanded a public inquiry before the scandal broke.
Enron's accounting fraud was concealed internally for a long time, causing thousands of employees to lose pensions before exposure.
Enron collapsed because of poor government regulation rather than internal fraud.
Enron's failure was discovered immediately, allowing most employees to be protected.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses Enron to show that delayed exposure increases the cost to ordinary people — employees lost pensions precisely because the fraud was hidden internally.
mcQuestions.hint: The Enron example illustrates what happens when exposure comes too late.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'to act without facing any punishment or consequence'?
options: to be held to account
to act with impunity
to demand structural change
to be shielded from scrutiny
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Act with impunity' means to do something wrong without facing consequences — the Latin root 'impunitas' means 'without punishment'.
mcQuestions.hint: The prefix 'im-' means 'without', and 'punity' relates to punishment.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's closing sentence 'Visibility is the condition of accountability, and accountability is the condition of trust' argue?
options: Trust can only be built through secrecy and careful management of information.
Accountability and trust are desirable but unrelated to how visible institutions are.
For institutions to be trusted, they must be accountable — and accountability requires that failures are visible, not hidden.
Trust in institutions depends mainly on the quality of their leaders, not on transparency.
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing statement chains three concepts (visibility → accountability → trust) to argue that transparency is the foundation of everything that makes institutions legitimate.
mcQuestions.hint: Trace the logical chain: visibility enables X, which enables Y.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition signals the essay's pivot from concession back to its main argument?
options: Granted,
Even so, the alternative has its own costs, and these tend to be paid by the public.
In conclusion,
For example,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the conceded damage from public failure and pivots to the argument that the alternative (concealment) is worse.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the transition that directly follows the concession paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'to be swept under the carpet' as a metaphor?
options: The fraud was swept under the carpet for years before it finally came to light.
The fraud swept under the carpet years before it came to light.
The carpet swept the fraud under it for many years before light.
Under the carpet the fraud was sweeping for years to come to light.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To be swept under the carpet' is a passive idiom meaning to hide something embarrassing — it requires the passive auxiliary 'was/is swept'.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a passive idiom — 'was swept under the carpet' not 'swept under the carpet'.

options: A financial investment in a publicly listed company
A personal stake in an outcome, especially one that might bias judgment
A formal agreement between two institutions
An academic qualification in economics or finance
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Vested interest' refers to a personal or financial stake in an outcome that makes a person or group likely to act in their own favour rather than impartially.
mcQuestions.hint: A 'vested' interest is one that is already established — you have something to gain or protect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses 'at the expense of' to describe a trade-off?
options: a managed concealment that protects reputations at the expense of reform
a managed concealment that protects reputations in the expense of reform
a managed concealment that protects reputations with the expense of reform
a managed concealment at the expense for reputations protecting reform
mcQuestions.explanation: 'At the expense of X' means something is gained while X is sacrificed — the preposition 'at' is fixed in this idiom.
mcQuestions.hint: The correct preposition in this idiom is 'at', not 'in', 'with', or 'for'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains B2 formal register throughout?
options: Banks getting caught is good because now everyone knows they were being dodgy.
The failure was catastrophic. It was also necessary.
Lol banks collapse and then everyone is surprised even though it was obvious.
Public failure is bad but like, it's better than not knowing I guess.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses a two-sentence contrast that is economical, precise, and controlled — a rhetorical technique appropriate for B2 argumentative writing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for precision, controlled tone, and no colloquialisms.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'to demand that someone explains or justifies their actions'?
options: to act with impunity
to conceal
to be held to account
to unravel
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To be held to account' means to be required to explain one's actions and accept responsibility for them — the opposite of acting with impunity.
mcQuestions.hint: Who is doing the 'holding' here? The public, a regulator, or a court.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's argument about 'private failure'?
options: Private failure is preferable because it protects innocent employees from publicity.
Private failure resolved quietly produces no lasting change because it creates no lasting public pressure.
Private failure is always more honest than public failure.
Private failure is effective at reforming institutions when handled correctly.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly argues that private failure 'produces no lasting change because it creates no lasting pressure' — the incentive structures that caused the failure remain intact.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the second paragraph's argument about why concealment fails to produce reform.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word correctly completes this sentence: 'The fraud was _____ for years before regulators acted'?
options: conceded
concealed
conferred
contrived
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Concealed' means hidden or kept secret — the correct word for fraud that is kept from public view.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'conceal' as 'hide' — the root relates to covering something.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of an institution you interact with — a school, a bank, a government office. If it were failing in some way, would you want to know publicly, or would you prefer a quiet internal fix? What does your answer reveal about your trust in that institution?
